scenario: Orion Carrier publishes 7000 events per day in Camunda 8.8 from a shipping platform and developers need to allow these messages to arrive before the matching catch event activates by configuring the time-to-live on the publish-message command in the v2 messages endpoint.
question: Which behavior does setting a positive time-to-live on a published message enable in Camunda 8 message buffering?

options.text: The message is delivered repeatedly every TTL milliseconds until a subscription accepts it, simulating an at-least-once notification pattern between systems
The message is buffered for the TTL duration and can be correlated to a subscription that opens later within that window on the same broker node
The message is broadcast to every active process instance with a matching definition once the TTL expires at the end of the buffering window on the broker
The message is held in a transactional outbox on the publisher and resent on each timer tick of the TTL until the broker acknowledges it back to the caller

a.text: Incorrect. TTL does not trigger periodic redelivery; it specifies a window during which the buffered message can be correlated to a subscription that opens later.

b.text: Correct. The Message buffering section states a message has a TTL that specifies for how long it is buffered, and within this time the message can be correlated to a process instance.

c.text: Incorrect. Messages are not broadcast at TTL expiry; the buffer is polled when a subscription opens, and the first published matching message is correlated in FIFO order.

d.text: Incorrect. The TTL governs broker-side buffering, not publisher retransmission; there is no documented outbox-based resend mechanism inside the messages page on TTL.

explanation: Message buffering lets the broker hold a published message for the supplied TTL. When a matching subscription opens within that window, it polls the buffer and the first published matching message (FIFO order) is correlated. A TTL of zero disables buffering and discards unmatched messages immediately.
